speaker: interviewer
text: --2, 1996, you were starting to tell us about the end, December 19-- 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, we heard in the camp [SIGHS] there is going to be an evacuation. And we were in [INAUDIBLE] still with a guard outside. You know, sonderkommado. So [SIGHS] they started assembling in the-- [SIGHS] in the-- the scharfuhrer, which was another [? commander, ?] he says, you don't have to worry about because for us a truck will come, for the people, [INAUDIBLE] people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other-- the-- they were leftover, the-- the chefs from the kitchen and the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], you know the rest of it, were already organizing for the transport. That was, they were ready to-- to-- to evacuate. What were they to do? Well, that's what it is. I knew the end will come, whatever. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Unexpectedly-- unexpectedly, there was an announcement, everybody has to leave, everybody-- the-- the chefs and the-- the-- the-- the-- blockalteste, and the-- the-- everybody has to leave the camp. And they opened up our block. At the time, we mixed up. We mixed up, and I held my hands to heaven. I couldn't believe it, but that's-- that's what it is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- they-- it took till 12 o'clock. There was a lot of people in Birkenau. It was a lot of-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we started marching. They marched out, and I was already with other people. I was with one guy from-- from my block, Schlomo Dragon. He was also my [? commando. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlomo Dragon. Schlomo Dragon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you start my marching, let me ask you a couple of questions. You remember the date of the uprising by the Jewish holiday. How did you keep time, how did you keep dates?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I keep dates?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know when it was, how did you know it was that holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew Purim, we knew. We knew. We knew. We had a guy in there. We had Jews there, some of them even put on [? tefillin ?] there. So there was no way of not knowing. They-- they-- they-- they came in-- German papers came in, there would be people who read from the-- from the other camps. It wasn't-- It wasn't in a jungle, so we-- we knew it was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever form a minyan--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the barrack? Did you ever pray in the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I interrupt, I bring something else out. When I was brought back-- when I was brought back from Auschwitz to Birkenau to block two, before we went to 13, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of a sudden I heard the night shift, which was bunker still night shift. They worked in bunker. I was coming from-- from-- from crematorium and I wasn't going with them. But bunker two came back at night shift, 12 o'clock, and they said there was a transport from [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had some sandwiches. I recognized the sandwiches from [INAUDIBLE]. The way the bread was packed. And my system with the kids were there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in this-- in this-- in this transport, in the-- in the-- in the-- in the bunker. And this was Purim. Erev Purim, was it? And we sat and sing inside the Purim, you know, in-- in-- in the camp. I remember even the song, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Know what I mean, the song? And it was for Purim and my sister was there. This was-- this was Purim. Other memories of that nature that happened during your time at Birkenau, other thing-- about the barracks. Didn't it get-- the Sonderkommando-- did more and more people die, was it lighter by the time you left-- lighter of people, fewer people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you that they took out as-- as-- as transports rose, they brought in new people. As transports fell down, they eliminated people. They set all kinds of tricks, but we knew the tricks. There was no way out. It was only a way out from-- from-- from there. But-- but that's why when-- when I was-- when I went to-- to clean up the crematorium from-- from-- from the [? cipher ?] there, I saw them working to the [? Sonder. ?] And it was-- and at that time, they-- they read the [INAUDIBLE], you know the numbers, from-- from-- from the beginning, not from the end. So my name was-- was mentioned there. This-- this is how it is. I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people-- how many people do you think survived from the day that you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- well, how did I survive? I didn't come to that. While we left at 12 o'clock, we went out from Auschwitz, from Birkenau on the way. And there was farm, a [INAUDIBLE] farm in-- in the-- with what do you call it? Where the straw is, where the [INAUDIBLE] is, you know I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barn to rest. In the morning, they assembled us. It was-- [INAUDIBLE] was out of Auschwitz, Rybnik. Rybnik. It was all the vicinity from Auschwitz and Chrzanow, you know what I mean? This-- this-- This is-- this is my home-- how my-- my home turf. And in the morning, they assembled us, and the SS men there with bayonets, just to-- to test, you know, in the straw, to test and-- and took us on the march. They didn't take the whole-- who camp, it was maybe 20-- 20,000 or something. But but whoever made it, marched, marched. Whoever was behind, they didn't bother with him. That's all. But then they-- they finished him off and they came a truck where they then put him up on top of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I, I was walking with this Schlomo Dragon. I said-- it's in-- in the middle of the-- it was-- was a dirt road, but snow. On the side was a ravine, you know? And there was fields with snow. And some here and there, peasants worked. So I said-- and-- and the SS was going apart. I says to the-- to Schlomo, you know what? This is the only chance now-- now. And we saw there an empty lawn and after the lawn was on a little forest. So I said to Schlomo, you know what? I was dressed in a [INAUDIBLE], a hat-- he, too. By the way, he was-- he was a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what that means, a [NON-ENGLISH]? He forked out food. He went to the kitchen, brought it in. He was a [NON-ENGLISH], but a nice guy. So I said-- I pulled him by-- by-- by the sleeve, and we went out the road into the ravine and we went further. And I said to myself, whatever happens will happen. What's the difference, now happen or tomorrow happen? And we reached the forest. And over there, we didn't have where to go, so we rested there. And the-- the frost was a biting frost and the night came in, and we heard the movement of the Germans off the road, moving. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, they-- they were also moving. The front was moving. And I was-- I thought I was-- I'm going-- I'm going to be-- to be a piece of ice. Anyway, so we went further. We started walking, and we went-- I-- I had no-- no-- no way of stopping it, so I knocked on a door. It was a-- a railroad man, worker. And he somehow smelled out that we are from the-- and the front was outside. The front we heard-- we heard the-- the-- the shooting from outside. He said, oh, yes. Yes. Yes. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While he-- So then he says, excuse-- he went out, he said, excuse me, I have to do this and this. I says to him, I don't like it. I just don't like it. It was in the-- late in the evening. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Going-- going a little further, we saw that he's coming back with a [POLISH], a police, you know, to get us out, a Polack. So we took the way, the road, and we went to the village of Chrzanow, Chrzanow On the way to Chrzanow, there's [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] was-- was-- was known to what it-- it was a-- a manufacturer of shoes. It was known, like here Florsheim. It was [INAUDIBLE]. But there was the [INAUDIBLE] and the next-- next little village was Libiaz, also. And we had customers in Libiaz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- while we walked this forest, we saw already there something which was-- it wasn't-- it wasn't like it's supposed to be. We saw barrels of sand from both sides. Nobody walks-- no-- no-- no-- no man's land. By the end-- by the-- by the end of the road coming into Libiaz there, we were stopped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Where are you going? What is this here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is-- this was-- this was forbidden to walk there. But we found out there was a Howitzer-- you know a Howitzer, a-- a-- a cannon. By the end of the road was a cannon and there's the front there. And I just came into the front. Anyway, I walked from one man to the other and explained to him that I had my sister in Libiaz. You know, I have a sister living in Libiaz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not-- he didn't-- I got a winter hat and I got a-- a three-quarter coat. And so if he would pull off the hat, he would see I'm shaven, but he didn't see it. So one to the other until we came to the end of the-- of the road, where the Howitzer was there, and it was hidden with-- under the trees. And he gave us permission to go to the sister. [INAUDIBLE]. So outside were a sled-- was a sled. And there were there were [NON-ENGLISH]. You know [NON-ENGLISH]? Silesia, it was-- the-- the Germans there were [NON-ENGLISH]. They were-- they were living on the border between Germany and Poland, was-- they called us [NON-ENGLISH] because Silesia was-- was-- was half Poland and-- [INAUDIBLE] was half Poland, half German. So they spoke Polish. So he explained it to me, you are lucky. You are lucky, you know? You are lucky. You went to [? no man's ?] [? land here. ?] OK. So we came to Libiaz. I-- I was glad that didn't ask me where-- where I want to go. I went down, I came into this-- to this guy, a customer, [INAUDIBLE], a nice guy. I came in into him. I said, Mr. This-and-this. When he saw me, he started trembling, I didn't mean to hurt you. But this is [INAUDIBLE] life. You know? I-- I-- I risk all my family here. Here is a soup. Here is bread, but go. And I did. And we were walking-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all the time till we came to Chrzanow. To Chrzanow, we to the town. So I was home. But the Germans were still there. So I went-- I was on my-- my home turf, I knew how to go so nobody could see me. And I went through little streets there till there was-- was a-- a-- a live-- a livable alley like, like, like, like, like-- live the table, four corners, you know? All around [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, there were stables. The people wasn't there, the Jews went there. But the Germans made from their houses stables. It was only one floor up, in poor-- a poor neighborhood. But nobody was there. I said to him, you know what, let's lay down. [LAUGHS] I laid down and I rested a little bit. But it was very cold. The windows were broken, nothing was open, no heat. Nothing was-- was there. No heat, no nothing. But we rested a little bit. I said, let's go further. Then I went further with-- with-- with those narrow streets and I came to another guy who knew my brother, who he went with him to school. And he was also a customer. He-- he made in the [? leather. ?] So I knocked on his door and he let me in. And he says, you stay here. So we went in-- in the main floor on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was stairs up up there and we went up. And we saw this was on the back of his window was a big field. And this field was bordering all the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what that means? All those shoes from Chrzanow. But there was no more shoes. But we-- but I saw-- I-- I knew this neighborhood like my hand. It was [INAUDIBLE] there. It was my shtiebel there. All-- all this here. This guy was living there. So I heard the shooting back and forth. All of a sudden, 1 o'clock, I heard marching. So he went to his wife and kids to the basement, because he had-- he had-- because of the war. And I had a feast. I went up and looked through the window what they're doing. It-- it-- it was music to my ears. Around 1 o'clock, noon time, they moved out to through little street where-- where I was there in this house, moved out to the main thing. We lived on the [INAUDIBLE], I told you that. I went to the school there. On this-- on this way, I told you before that I was in the-- in a-- in a-- in a-- what do you call it? An-- an airplane bombing, in-- in-- where I-- where I went to the school there. So what do you call it here? When the planes come to bomb? What-- what-- An air raid.

speaker: interviewee
text: An air raid. Yeah, air raid. So everybody was running. So I was running with him, too, to a-- to a bunker, to a-- to a cellar. All of a sudden, when I was sitting with-- with Schlomo Dragon in the cellar, I recognized a guy who went with me to school. His father was-- he wasn't bad, he was a-- a-- a fireman. [INAUDIBLE], I remember the name. He saw me. I saw him. Maybe he didn't recognize me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I saw already the-- the air was-- was-- the air was-- was thick. [LAUGHS] I says, you know what, Schlomo? Let's go. I went out. So the guy went-- the ammunition was there with-- with a rifle. But I went out. This was-- this was from there, I went out to this guy, which he-- he take me and this other guy. And from there, I saw the Russians-- the-- the Germans moving out. A half an hour, the Russians were in. This was the end. What was your state of health by this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My state of health? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some-- you-- you never got sick during the whole time you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your strength, you felt you could keep going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I survived. But I wasn't a muselmann, you know what I mean? As long-- one thing which I can-- I can tell you is I didn't suffer hunger. You know what I mean? I didn't suffer hunger. There was-- there was something to eat. This keep me alive. But I didn't expect anything. I didn't expect anything because nobody from there came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the end of it was, when I was-- after the-- after the-- the war, when I met people who went with it-- because there were, what, 70 people or 50 people when-- when-- with me when we evacuated? I-- I met some people, and they told me when we went out from the road, everybody was looking. And the SS was looking. They didn't know what we looking, but nobody said a word. So everybody was-- they were looking, they were looking, and then he told us-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When it came to Germany, Sachsenhausen-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was far away from Auschwitz, but all of a sudden-- all of a sudden, they called out, who is from Sonderkommando? Who is from Sonderkommando? Who is the Sonderkommando? What is this, they're going to give you an order, want to get a medal? Who-- who is from Sonderkommando? But somehow, I guess nobody-- nobody was-- was coming forward. But-- but this was already-- this was already end of-- end of April, May and I was liberated in January, January 19, exactly two years to the day, you know? Two years to the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if I would go further, plenty of people died by the march. In-- in-- but Sonderkommando, there was still a danger wherever they come. And so So that they didn't want us out. They had no-- no way of-- of-- of helping this here. That's what the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept your health because you say you had enough food. How did you keep your mind? What did you think about? What kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] should I tell you? I'll tell you this. People are stronger than anything. You adjust to any situation. If you know that the end of the road comes, you live this way, then-- then you have a way of living it. I can tell you an example. There was transport from Greeks, Greeks, you know, small people. They-- they were-- you know what their job was? Their job was stomping-- stomping the ashes, stomping the ashes. And stomping the ashes was a-- was a dirty job because it was hot, hot ashes. And you had to-- you had to-- you had to take them out when they're hot, stomp them when they're hot. And-- and-- it burns all around you, the smell, you know, for the lungs. And they were working and singing. They knew what what to do. They knew their end. They were singing. And then they make-- they made also like-- like a little show. They made a show about this-- I told you in the papers there was one [INAUDIBLE]. We called him the [NON-ENGLISH]. He was always coming when there was a transport, you know, to bring the good news. So this guy, this Greek, he-- he-- he simulated him. [LAUGHS] He's is coming in, 1,000 people, 500 people, you live with it. That's the way it is. I don't know how, but that's the way it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people help each other out in the Sonderkommando? Because you were closed off from everyone else. Did people share food or share whatever they got?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten inside at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The work was what they wanted you to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Sonderkommando, nobody was beaten, one from the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but even from the kapos, from the SS that came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The SS? I told you about [? Moll. ?] Moll was-- was-- was-- [? Moll ?] and-- and-- and [INAUDIBLE], he once catched me when they took off the clothes outside. Because the mortuary was full of dead, of bodies. So from-- from the outside, when they took off the clothes, they took him to the bunkers. So before they took the clothes on the trucks, there was food and I took food. And [INAUDIBLE], the one who first told [INAUDIBLE], I have enough, you take it over, he saw me there. He-- he took the handle from my stick. He gave me a [INAUDIBLE]. I still have here a hole here, from that time. If you can see it here on top-- on top of the head. So they weren't gentle. They weren't gentle. But by all means, that's-- this is-- listen, if you adapt yourself and you have no other way, unless-- unless you-- you want to commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe tomorrow will be better. Everybody hopes. And the time came. And your-- your sense of your religion, your sense of your belief, how was that affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It comes back. It comes back. My kids are all in yeshivas, and I belong to Young Israel and I'm a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what you call it? [INAUDIBLE], you know what I mean? Somehow. It's what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: But inside, did you think about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside there? I didn't think nothing. I didn't think nothing. This-- this-- this dying, he tried everything here and there. What's-- what's-- what's there to say, [INAUDIBLE], you know what I mean? This is-- this is what should I say, what should [? I eat? ?] [INAUDIBLE]. So this is the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were working all day, did you keep your mind occupied with something or you just concentrated on your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept plenty-- plenty of times of my mind-- my sister with the kids, the parents, the other sister, the other sister. The other sister was in Plaszow, you know, [INAUDIBLE] Krakow, the one who brought me food, a package. She was still in Krakow and her husband was coming-- he was in Tarnov. [INAUDIBLE] he came home to her. And she was also living with us, but then when she had the baby, she-- she had to go to Krakow. So what do I want to say?

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of what you were thinking about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was thinking about the family. Plenty of times I was thinking about the family. He-- she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her husband to get her to-- to-- they-- they evacuated all Krakow, the ghetto, at that time, and they sended-- sent the young people to Plaszow, whatever, to the camp. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now that [INAUDIBLE] I forget, I black out, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right. They-- you're talking about your daughter-- your sister that was send with the baby to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with-- and with her husband's mother was also the there. Mother and she with the baby and her sister. So the Polack went out and brought them-- and he left them outside by a Polack and he went into the camp to make connections to go to Hungary, which he did. But Hungary-- but-- from Hungary to Romania, he did. But before he did, he had in mind to bring the family, too. But the Polack went out and brought in the Germans and they-- and they made-- they-- they made-- they [INAUDIBLE]. By the way, I have the pictures to here when I was in Poland and I was [INAUDIBLE], which-- which he he brought her out after the war. He knew where she was buried because she wasn't at the cemetery. He brought her to the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her husband survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: He doesn't live now. Husband survived, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened-- you said your grandmother lived with you. Did she live till the war time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My grandmother lived to-- she died in 1930-- 1930, 10 years before. And died. Yeah? What?  Ok.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's close this tape and we'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
